transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Hello?
Hello?
Hi, sorry about that.
Hi.
How are you?
I'm doing well.
How are you?
I'm good.
Sorry.
I'm just, when you hopped off, I got a Slack message, and so let me just respond to it.
No worries.
No worries.
Okay.
All right.
Hi.
Sorry.
Nice to meet you.
How's it going?
Nice meeting you as well.
It's going all right.
I'm in central Wisconsin, northwest Wisconsin, and it's chilly and beautiful.
I'm excited that winter's coming.
How are you?
Amazing.
I have a friend that's out there for work this week, and she sent me a picture, and it looks like it's snowed already?
Yep.
First snow, I think, yesterday or the day before a little.
Oh my gosh, crazy.
It sounds like I might get some later this week, so.
Where are you based out of?
I'm in Rhode Island.
Oh, awesome.
Yeah.
Yeah, so New England area.
The smallest state, right?
Yes, the smallest state.
Lots of attitude to make up for it, I think.
That's awesome.
Well, I can kick things off by giving you a little bit of background on me and the role.
We can talk about you for most of the time.
And then leave some time for questions at the end.
Very standard setup here.
But, and like, I think we have 45 minutes, but usually we take about 30.
So, you know, we can go as short as you need to.
But the, so I've been at GrowthX for about seven months now, but we're pretty young company.
We're just hit our one year.
We're just doing our first renewals over the last few months.
So, you know, still very much in growth phase, figuring things out.
And I lead customer operations, which is interesting in this org, because right now it means I'm managing kind of our full delivery organization.
We're set up as a services, we're in the services space right now.
Essentially a marketing agency, but we have a platform on the back end that...
We use to generate all of our content using AI workflows.
So that will eventually become externally facing and be like a product offering that people can leverage at different price points.

And so I think the interesting thing about what we're doing right now is we're kind of dogfooding this external product, but able to have a really stable revenue and growth rate, even considering that the product is not like live to the public yet and allows us to really refine that product.
And we keep building on it.
So some really cool things in the works.
But my background's in CS.
CS, I am managing kind of like the how of putting everything together so that we can meet client deliverables, measure client health.
Think about a lot of my time right now is spent capacity planning because we're taking on like two clients a week and trying to scale efficiently.
And so what we're looking for is someone who can help me get out of the weeds a little bit and put a lot of the plans into practice that we're thinking about right now.
So one of the big projects that we put on pause a couple weeks ago is we were thinking about we've got a linear system.
If you've used JIRA, it's very similar.
That is we're having kind of our client teams put together like tickets of what they're working on sort of as a project tracking tool.
We also use Notion, but would be great to have something that integrates with HubSpot, helps us kind of have like more standardized health scoring.
We have like health scoring, ICP scoring, but something that kind of helps with project tracking.
And all of those things in one.

So somebody that can kind of implement that, think about analytics, and a lot of just like the moving the pieces on the board as we start to make plans and expand our operations to essentially a product launch as well.
So lots of things going on.
think one of the exciting things about this role is that there's so much green space.
So, you know, somebody asked me, somebody else who's interviewing asked me about like, what's career pathing look like?

And I don't want to be prescriptive on that because I think like, it's, if you are running after a problem and it makes you really excited and you can see like how we grow it, like that's the way that this business is working right now.
And so I think there's a lot of opportunity there.
Yeah, absolutely.
I mean, at the girl stage, the very nascent motion of dogfooding a product before even taking that product to the public and just doing it from like, ultimately, what will be your.
Client's experience using this product is such an interesting point of growth.
So very exciting.
Awesome.
Well, why don't you tell me a little bit about yourself?
Absolutely.
I have been working in operations supporting customer-facing teams for about over the last 10 years.
The first five was in consumer package good manufacturing.
I made the switch to B2B SaaS about six and a half, seven years ago.
I have progressively gone smaller in the company sizes that I've been targeting.
I do love the interesting challenges of a startup.
So I started at Jamf way back in 2019 and cut my teeth, if you will, insofar as customer operations in SaaS, program management, and then systems management.
I fell in love with systems, cross-platform data integration, and really opted...
Optimizing processes to make the work that people do inside the organization easier, more effective, more efficient.
I was there for a couple of years and then was recruited by a company called Drift, which was at the time a unicorn, a newly minted unicorn in the startup world.

And I implemented their GameSite instance, their customer success platform, and brought in a bunch of foundational processes for their already existing customer success team and formalized the motion for them, as well as overhauled their survey programs.
I was only there a short while because once again, I was recruited by Apollo.
Apollo was even a smaller company.
I joined around, I think, the 200-person mark with Apollo, and I was the first hire in their customer success org.
They were a product-led growth model through and through until the summer of 2022 when they decided to launch a customer success team and begin.
Moving up market.
So I was hired at the same time as a people leader within customer success.

And I built and scaled all of the systems, processes, and data flows for that net new customer success org and partnered with the people leader in, you know, rolling all of that out, hiring up a team.
within six months, we had a team of 15 or so customer success folks supporting our managed books as a reward for that.
They gave me the support world as well.
So I inherited a broken instance of Zendesk, a broken instance of intercom, and just an absolute mess to untangle of KPIs.
But once again, I love a mess.
Like my brain thrives on that stuff in creating order from the chaos, simplifying internal processes, and cleaning house around systems.
So I ultimately there, I deprecated our Zendesk instance, we had duplicity left, right and center.
So we had our tickets going, our email tickets through Zendesk.
Our chats through intercom, none of the metrics, none of the capacity planning was possible.
I got everything into intercom, which was more than just the support org because marketing was here and security was there and stuff.
So it was just this realignment and found success there.

So it was ultimately saved on the tooling, improved the processes, gave so much more clarity to executive leadership, all the way down to like OKR goals for the team leads as to like how their pod of people was performing.
So I really, really enjoyed my time at Apollo.
I've been on a sabbatical from the corporate world for the last year and a half.
My partner is a business owner with two distinct business units.
So I built him a website.
I'm doing all of his retail work.
He's an artist.
Some of the art back here is his.
And yeah, I've really enjoyed my time off and, but I'm ready to get back into it.
I need to scratch that itch.
I want to go on vacation.
I mean.
Money when I'm working.
So I want to do that again.
My husband is also a small business owner and yeah, like spent some time away from work last year.
So, or beginning of this year.
So very much feel that it's very re-energizing, but then you're like, I really like working.
There's so much I can do.
Yeah.
Awesome.

Well, I would love to like learn a little bit more about either the Gainsight implementation or another project that you like built end to end, the process involved, and then kind of how you got buy-in and got people to like adopt a new process too.
Absolutely.
Um, I'll pull one that I, I didn't really cover, but when I stood up the customer success org at Apollo, um, I, I joined and we didn't have any, they didn't hire me with a
Tooling budget whatsoever.
They're like, you're going to use Salesforce and whatever tools we have in the go-to-market stack.

So I came in and I, in short order, like a week and a half, did a customer journey map where I pulled together what I could on my own and then did stakeholder interviews to understand like, what is the customer experience?
Who is talking to the customers?
On what surfaces?
Is it a pop-up in app from the product team?
Is it a marketing email?
That sort of thing.
Where can I fit this customer success org?
So we're not over-in-in-dating, but still providing value.
And then identified like the tool set and the data flows.
And I realized that we needed a customer success platform.
So I got preliminary buy-in from my direct boss at the time, the SVP of RevOps, and was like, I would like to embark on this discovery mission.
And I began trialing three different CSPs, catalyst, plan, hat, and vitally, which I ultimately implemented and had a matrix of needs.

App analysis to fill, chose vitally, and then I got buy-in from like the VP of support, the customer success team I was supporting, the systems team, like all of these other higher than me level stakeholders gave me their soft buy-in so that I could ultimately package up a business proposal and take it directly to the desk of the CEO and present to him and say like, I need to spend $60,000 on this bit of software and this is why you will agree with me.
And he agreed.
So then I went through and I think it took me six weeks or four to six weeks.
I implemented vitally according to all of the business requirements that we had set out in alignment with the goals for our customer success org.
That makes me really excited because we literally canceled the meeting with vitally today.
We'd like demoed it.
They keep wanting us to do a trial and do the implementation.
And I'm like, I want this, but.
We need somebody that's going to, like, have the time to devote to implementing it.
So you're actually making me feel good that we were, like, on the right track.
Yeah, no, Vitaly is great.
It's all about finding the right tool for your company.
So I have extensive experience in Gainsight, but for a smaller org, absolutely not.
Absolutely not.
Yeah.
What were the, like, KPIs that you guys were using to measure success with the customer success org?
I mean, for, like, the implementation and rollout.
implementation.
Sorry, sorry.
Yeah.
So implementation, I would say, in tandem with the people leader, or the people side of it, like, we went from, like, zero named accounts.
So my customer success manager was just getting pulled into random calls because a salesperson was like, this is a $600,000 account.
She'd be, like, trying to catch up.
So having a name on the.
That was owned by CS.
went from zero to like the first 150.
And then from that 150, like six months later, we had 4,000 named accounts under CS ownership, which facilitated the handoffs and all of those things.
Implementation wise, I mean, I had a project proposal and then knocking off all of like the integrations, the playbook builds.
So covering the key areas of onboarding and getting, we did have a few dedicated team members to the onboarding process.
Getting them rolling and then tracking their project progression throughout and their utilization.
That was part of it.

And then once we had our first like two additional CSMs, getting their renewal forecasts and like number of accounts and dollar amount value within the next nine or 120, 90, 60, 30 was an early iteration short or a quick follow to that was like a health score.

That went along with it, building a health score based on what we had for product utilization correlation to success and getting just something down so that we could understand the general health of our customers.
And moving from the product-led growth into SaaS, I came in and had hundreds of thousands of customer records that were actively paying.
So when I say mess, I mean like mess.
So I deployed the most logical to me, like first principle theories to pull to the top those accounts that we needed to service now and then build out the processes in tandem.
I would give those accounts to those CSFs and then build a tandem.
I will say that we did hit some hiccups with our onboarding team for this rollout.
They were set in their ways and they didn't have a strong leader at the time to go.
So it was difficult to get them to adopt what I laid out for project management.
In retrospect, something I've taken away from that specific experience is building a lighter framework to begin with and iteratively adding to it.
In my time at Jamf and at Drift, I was working with teams that were so used to full-fledged projects and programs that they would quickly adopt to multi-step, multi-layer kind of frameworks.
But my experience at Apollo showed me like in a scrappy startup, sometimes teams really just need iterative step-by-step guidance when it comes to adopting a net new process.

So I would have started with just, you need to write some notes every time you're on the phone call with a customer rather than check off this box and this box and this box every time you're on the phone with a customer.
Yeah.
If that makes sense.
Yeah.
That makes a lot of sense.
Cool.
So taking a step back, you mentioned that when you started, you kind of did a customer journey map and figured out like what needed to be done.
How did you decide like which operational problems you needed to solve first?
Absolutely.
I wrote down everything I could.
I have experience in customer journey mapping.
I'm not saying I'm an expert, but I'm pretty darn good at it and then use my materials, right?
So I documented everything and identified where the gaps were really.
So where we didn't have coverage for like renewals, where our onboarding program kind of like flatlined, leaving customers of high value twisting in the wind, that sort of thing.

And also the lapses in handoff motions, where a customer might just like hit an organic dead end because we as the company, the vendor of goods did not identify that we had created a dead end mid product.
mid-life cycle, customer life cycle.
So yeah, I wrote down everything I could and then tried to find those gaps.
And yeah, it was fun.
Yeah.
And then how did you make a decision on like what's tackle first?
Dollar value.
It was really like customer segmentation wise.
The goal was to go upmarket and provide a more white glove service to those upmarket customers that we had already somehow brought on through our cobbled together program.
So focusing on making sure they were successful and retaining them.
And they were, we had a batch of renewals every single month that we were working with.
So it was a constant, a constant fire drill for a couple of months there.
Yeah.
Yeah.
That's where we are.
Okay, cool.
Talk to me about a time when you had to work through conflicting priorities.
Yeah, there was a bit of an overlap in the time I was director of CX Ops at Apollo.
So I was hiring on a new CX Ops manager to continue the work that I had started with creating that nascent team.
And meanwhile, the support team was like, come here, work with us now, right?
So we had brought on even more CS leadership.
Our team was up to like 25 people at this time.
I had a net new CX Ops person that I was trying to ramp up as fast as I could.
And support was like, knock, knock, knock, knock, knock.
First response times are over an hour in chat.
Help, help, help.
And I'm like, okay, okay.
So I had to trust.
And I hired an amazing CX Ops manager.
So I know I trusted well.
But strategically, we had just hired a new CRO.
So we were midstream launching a scale team.
CS kind of component to our programming to help cover the long tail, midstream on the work there.
My CS ops manager was running in that direction.
I was doing a system evaluation of intercom to try to correct our chat first response time.
Meanwhile, CRO comes in, throws a wrench, and says, we are no longer doing the scaled CS.
And I had to go with my gut and just let my CS ops manager make director level decisions on the fly, because I had to focus.
So it's really just trusting the team you have and doing what you can with the time you have.
So, I mean, I was in an intensive two-week turnaround where I got our first response time over chat from over an hour to under a minute.

And in the meantime, I was, you know, during our one-to-ones and ad hoc daily meetings, working with her to realign our entire operational roadmap to support this customer success team who just got a new CRO two levels up that decided that we needed to change our direction overall.
Wow.
Wow.
I mean, you figure it out.
You find a way.
Sometimes you work long hours.
It is what it is.
Yeah, yeah.
I'm curious, like, how have you built trust with, like, non-direct reports or other teams when you've been working through a project where there might be resistance?
Absolutely.
All the time.
Being a real person to the core, being kind, courteous, and respectful.
I always try to put myself in other people's shoes.
I've been that person that literally doesn't have time to eat, much less answer this new hire's questions about a process that I wrote six months ago so that I can bring up speed, right?
So when I'm in either position, just lead with kindness and respect.

So if I'm asking for somebody's time or input and they communicate in some way, shape, or form that they might have been that version that I've been just way too much all of the time, asking who a good start it might be.
Doing, confirming, like, I've reviewed this wiki, this page of Notion, whatever it might be.
Is there anything you'd like to add or can I run with this?
And just putting it in a Slack and moving on, that sort of thing.
Meeting them where they're at.
Because it's not a problem if everybody has the time and bandwidth to work together.
But I found the most friction in that kind of scenario being when resources are spread thin, there's no real buy-in for this person to work with me, but I need their help.
Just being kind.
Awesome.
Cool.
Well, those are all of my questions.
What questions do have for me?
Yeah, I'd love to hear from you more about the team or teams you're supporting.
When I spoke with the recruiter, I'm sorry, I keep paper notes for some reason.
Oh, I have this one.
I have this one.
I lied.
When I spoke with the recruiter, they said something about, like, pods that have, like, between five and seven peeps.
Like what, what teams are you supporting?
What do these people do?
And what?
Tell me more, please.
Yeah, so I am managing, oh gosh, I just had it up here.
Like 40, 50 people right now.
So that's, we don't have like support.
It's all because it's all like marketing deliverables.
So organic blog content is really what it is.
So companies have like a blog they want to show for SEO purposes.
And so we generate a lot of blog content.
We have, because we're leveraging AI, our deliverables are like much more aggressive than most other agencies.
And we have teams of like four to five people supporting like four to five accounts.
We're at like 1.2 people per account right now.
Wow.
We're working.
Wow.

We're trying to get that, you know, to scale as we add more clients, so we're currently, like, re-orging a little bit to try and get it to 1.5, and that's just with, like, adding headcount and adding customers without adding more headcount is the real goal of the re-org.

So we're, I'm managing that, those teams, I think we're going to add a couple of manager layers, but we're very, like, widely distributed, and most of these, it's, like, content, so it's generating content on the platforms, putting out, like, a blog, editing it, and then getting it to the client and getting it published.
So it's a lot of, like, independent self-serve work, and, again, not, like, we have the platform, but the clients aren't using the platform, so not a lot of need for support at the moment.
So, as I said, a lot of my time is thinking about capacity right now, but we're, it's also managing.
And kind of like, what are the feedback loops?
How do we get visibility into like different teams capacities and how much time they're spending in the tool versus like, we're also leveraging like Claude and other like AI tools.
So kind of how are the teams using each tool?
That's where ideally like, it will all move into our internal platform and like, they'll be able to do kind of every step of the production process in the platform.
But we need to understand kind of how they're working, what they're getting done so that we can resource appropriately across all of the different clients.

And then I'm also responsible for tracking like the renewals and working with like the directors and account strategists who are managing these clients, like the relationship side of them to figure out like, okay, are they going to renew flat?
Are there other opportunities and think about how we pitch those and forecast?
But right now, it's just like most of it is flat just because we know that we're moving in a different direction in the near future.
And like the platform needs to be able to like do a couple of other things before we can offer additional work streams.
Okay.
That makes sense.
That makes sense.
What challenges are you hoping to overcome by implementing a tool like VitalLeaf?

So visibility into like, we've got a bunch of different places where we're tracking client health, where we're looking at like ICP scoring, where we're doing, we have a, like I said, we're using linear tickets right now to track like project management of like, what are you doing for this client?
Because sometimes there's like strategic alignment that need, that we need to get for buy-in for, you know, if we want to go in a different direction with the blog or we...
We need additional design resources, things like that.
That's happening in Linear, but then we have like health scoring in Notion, and it's all kind of like it's across the board.
And the health scoring is like self-reporting right now, so we don't have anything that's like pulling in.
It's just like, okay, if you were going to rate like these four pieces based on what's happened in the last week, how would you rate them?
Um, so I think like just trying to get one place where we can project manage, get visibility into who's assigned to which clients.
Like I'm doing my planning in Figma right now, um, but like the projects are somewhere else.

So it's just all in different places, and we wanted to be in one, like in a unified, on a unified platform, so that we can, um, have a better visibility into like understanding problem areas faster than having to like.
Like go through things manually and like have meetings on like twice a week just to be like, what's happening?
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yes.
That makes a ton of sense.
Thank you so much.
What's your, like, I know this is a new, new, new baby company, like BI team.
Do have a systems team?
What are you working with for resources?
Like what will this role, who will this role partner with?
Or is this like the pioneer?
Yeah.
So this is the pioneer.
We are, I think, thinking about hiring a data analyst at the same time.
And we just don't have like one thing at a time just because of our own resourcing.
And so I think like there will be a data analyst.
You, depending on what you need, I think like the chief of staff has been helping me with like, was helping me scope vitally.
He's like, worn a bunch of different hats in the org.
So he.
Has a good idea of, like, some of the other pieces that are needed for, like, CEO, like, C-suite visibility and things like that.
And then probably we do have, like, a contractor who does, like, all of our HubSpot sort of, like, configurations.
And he's basically full-time, I think.
I don't know.
I've never messaged him when he hasn't gotten back to me.
And then, like, probably finance a little bit, too.
But, yeah, like, I think we have room to hire another one to two people, depending on, but, like, we're kind of going to go one at a time to figure that out.
That makes sense.
That makes sense.
That's interesting.
I'm, like, interested.
So thank you for this conversation.
Because it sounds like so much fun.
What other questions do you have for me or what other information might I provide that would be helpful for you in your decision-making process?
I mean, I would love to know you talked a little bit about like how data excites you and you love to like run analytics.
So what's like a project on that end of the spectrum that you're super proud of?
Yeah, I would say in partnership with a fantastic BI org, but like walking into a mess, right?
So when I joined Apollo, we didn't have really fleshed out utilization milestones.
So when this, yeah, that we had some archaic data in that was attached to the contact or user level in our Salesforce records.
But like it, the refresh schedule was icky that we had some really rough aggregates that were rolling up to the account level in Salesforce.
But I couldn't get a justification as to like, what are the definitions behind this blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah.
So I'm seeking to understand one of the steps I took.
So and around the vitally implementation was to like talk to our product team and like, what does this mean?
And got this obscure notion page from years ago that explained it all.
And then really refine the data flows.
We had Snowflake and we were hiring up a BI team, so they weren't even there yet, but I think we had somebody on BI that knew Snowflake better than I do.
And they were able to like give me the raw data polls of like, this is the utilization data on the contact level and the account level that matters because we actually use these data flows are live.
They have one truncated by engineering and I got to pull those directly into vitally bypass Salesforce.
So I created a clean environment with a holistic view and then could create the health scores and stuff like that.
And then show like an executive level dashboard of all of our managed accounts, X amount are using sales engagement, X amount are using prospecting and other features and stuff.
So really bringing visibility to data that was tucked away in the data.
Like, or the product itself, and bringing that into an actionable place for CSMs all the way up to, like, decision makers.
Yeah.
Awesome.
I think you would have fun talking to our engineers who are building the platform.
Like, we don't even have PMs.
We just, our engineers are all, like, super senior, and they're, like, I'm to PM this myself for better or worse, for sure, for better or worse.
But, like, they also get super excited if they're, like, you're, like, I need this thing, and they're, like, oh, how do I do that?
Like, yeah.
And we do have Snowflake on the, like, on the platform side.
We just, because we think we're thinking about how we can build a bit more of a moat on, like, like, knowledge pulled directly into the platform.
So, yeah.
Very cool stuff.
Those are all the questions I had for you.
This was an amazing conversation.
Agreed.
Agreed.
Agreed.
Good luck in the hiring.
I hope to hear more from you and GrowthX.
You definitely will..ai or GrowthX?
It's GrowthX.ai.
We just call it GrowthX.
Okay, perfect.
Thank you.
I've been mixing up the terminology in my records.
So thank you.
Yeah, yeah.
Cool.
Well, I will talk to you again soon.
I think your next step will be to talk to Bridget, who's my boss.
She's the VP or something of RevOps.
I don't know which, what level of title we've gotten to at this point.
Okay.
But I think you'll have fun talking to her too.
Awesome.
Awesome.
Well, thank you so much for your time and have a good one.
Yes, you too.
Thanks, Leah.
Bye.
you.
